title: Mughlai chicken
subtitle: Chicken in a cream, almond, and saffron sauce
excerpt: Chicken thighs cooked in a rich, pale sauce of cream, ground almonds, yoghurt, and saffron with a base of slow-cooked spiced onion. The Mughal court dish that reached Anglo-Indian tables — aromatic, mild, and luxurious in texture.
sourceNotes: Based on Mughlai cooking traditions documented in Salma Husain, 'The Emperor's Table: The Art of Mughal Cuisine' (2008) and Madhur Jaffrey, 'A Taste of India' (1985). Mughlai cuisine is the cooking of the Mughal court of northern India, which shaped the entire tradition of restaurant Indian cooking across South Asia and the diaspora.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Sauce base can be made a day ahead. Add cream and finish just before serving.

content.text: Mughlai chicken belongs to the northern Indian court tradition of cooking that reached its height under the Mughal emperors of the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries. The defining characteristic of Mughlai cooking is richness: cream, clarified butter, dried fruit, nuts, and saffron applied with a careful hand. The dish is mild in heat but complex in flavour, built on a slow-cooked onion base and thickened with ground almonds rather than flour or lentils.

The technique requires patience at the onion stage: the onions must become deeply golden, almost caramelised, before anything else is added. This caramelised onion base (called browned onion paste in restaurant kitchens) is the foundation of the sauce and cannot be rushed.

items.prepNote: steeped in 2 tbsp warm milk for 10 minutes

content.text: Steep the {{saffron}} in 2 tablespoons of warm milk. Heat {{unsalted-butter}} of butter and {{sunflower-oil}} of oil in a Dutch oven over a medium heat. Add {{cardamom-pods}} lightly crushed cardamom pods, {{cloves}} cloves, and the {{cinnamon-stick}} — fry for 30 seconds. Add {{onion}} sliced onion and cook slowly for 20–25 minutes until deeply golden. Add {{garlic}} and {{ginger-fresh}} grated ginger and cook for 3 more minutes.

Add {{chicken-thigh}} of chicken thighs and stir to coat in the onion base. Cook for 5 minutes over a medium heat. Whisk {{full-fat-yoghurt}} of yoghurt with {{ground-almonds}} of ground almonds and add to the pan. Stir and reduce the heat to low. Add the saffron and its soaking milk. Cover and cook for 25–30 minutes until the chicken is cooked through, stirring occasionally. Stir in {{double-cream}} of double cream and {{garam-masala}} of garam masala. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Heat through gently for 3 minutes without boiling. Serve with basmati rice and warmed flatbread.

items.cause: Temperature too high when yoghurt is added
items.fix: Reduce the heat to low before adding the yoghurt-almond mixture and stir continuously. Full-fat yoghurt splits less readily than low-fat.

items.cause: Ground almonds not blended with yoghurt before adding
items.fix: Always whisk the ground almonds into the yoghurt before adding to the pan. This prevents them forming lumps.

items.cause: Ground almonds absorbing too much liquid
items.fix: Add a splash of warm water and stir. The sauce should be thick enough to coat the chicken but pourable.

content.text: Mughlai cooking took shape in the imperial kitchens of Delhi and Agra from the sixteenth century, when Persian and Central Asian culinary traditions merged with the spice palette of the Indian subcontinent. The result, rich, aromatic, and frequently cream and nut-enriched, became the template for restaurant Indian cooking across South Asia and, eventually, in Britain. Dishes like chicken tikka masala and korma are distant descendants of this tradition. Mughlai chicken as a named dish appears in Anglo-Indian cookery books of the twentieth century as a shorthand for this style: mild, fragrant, and associated with cooking for guests.
